findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is an expansile, lytic lesion centred within the posterior elements of the spine. The lesion demonstrates cortical thinning and focal cortical destruction, frequently resulting in an 'absent pedicle' sign on anteroposterior radiographs.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include extension into the vertebral body and potential encroachment into the epidural space, causing central canal or neural foraminal stenosis. Cross-sectional imaging classically reveals multiple thin-walled, cystic cavities containing fluid-fluid levels due to internal haemorrhage.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a dense osteoid matrix or extensive peritumoral bone marrow oedema extending far beyond the lesion to suggest an osteoblastoma. Furthermore, there is no aggressive permeative destruction affecting multiple levels typical of metastatic disease.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is a highly expansile, multicystic lytic lesion of the posterior elements containing characteristic fluid-fluid levels in a young patient.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Aneurysmal bone cyst, given the marked expansile nature and classic fluid-fluid levels representing blood degradation products of varying ages. The predilection for the posterior elements of the spine in children and young adults makes this the most likely primary diagnosis.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Aneurysmal bone cyst (ABC), however since this is already our primary consideration, the next most important differential would be Metastases. Lytic metastases typically occur in older patients, appear permeative and destructive, and lack the characteristic multiloculated fluid-fluid levels.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Osteoblastoma, given it also frequently arises in the posterior elements of the spine in young patients and its presentation can overlap with secondary ABCs. However, osteoblastomas typically demonstrate an osteoid matrix and are characteristically surrounded by extensive peritumoral oedema that extends well beyond the boundaries of the lesion.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to define the full anatomical extent of the lesion, specifically assessing for epidural extension, spinal cord compression, and the integrity of the vertebral body to prevent pathological fracture.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is essential to establish the growth rate of the lesion and identify any preexisting osseous lesions, as up to one-third of ABCs are secondary to another underlying bone tumour.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the whole spine, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely demonstrating the characteristic fluid-fluid levels of varying signal intensities and accurately evaluating any spinal cord or nerve root compression.
management.ctPhases: A non-contrast CT of the spine would be highly beneficial to precisely define the delicate bony septations, the extent of focal cortical destruction, and aid in surgical planning.
management.mriSequences: I would specify T1-weighted and T2-weighted sequences to demonstrate the fluid-fluid levels—where extracellular methaemoglobin in the supernatant is high signal on T1, contrasting with low signal intracellular methaemoglobin in dependent red blood cells—along with STIR sequences to assess local oedema.
management.intervention: If the diagnosis remains in doubt or a secondary underlying lesion is suspected, I would specify a CT-guided percutaneous biopsy, targeting the peripheral solid-tissue component or septations to avoid the non-diagnostic necrotic or haemorrhagic centre.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Spinal Surgery and Sarcoma MDT. My report would clearly document the degree of spinal canal stenosis, the presence of any cord compression, and the exact vertebral levels involved to guide definitive surgical curettage and spinal stabilisation.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is an expansile, lytic lesion centred within the posterior elements of the spine. The lesion demonstrates cortical thinning and focal cortical destruction, frequently resulting in an 'absent pedicle' sign on anteroposterior radiographs. Supporting features include extension into the vertebral body and potential encroachment into the epidural space, causing central canal or neural foraminal stenosis. Cross-sectional imaging classically reveals multiple thin-walled, cystic cavities containing fluid-fluid levels due to internal haemorrhage. Importantly, there is no evidence of a dense osteoid matrix or extensive peritumoral bone marrow oedema extending far beyond the lesion to suggest an osteoblastoma. Furthermore, there is no aggressive permeative destruction affecting multiple levels typical of metastatic disease. The feature that most influences my thinking is a highly expansile, multicystic lytic lesion of the posterior elements containing characteristic fluid-fluid levels in a young patient.

The appearances are most in keeping with Aneurysmal bone cyst, given the marked expansile nature and classic fluid-fluid levels representing blood degradation products of varying ages. The predilection for the posterior elements of the spine in children and young adults makes this the most likely primary diagnosis. The principle differential would be Aneurysmal bone cyst (ABC), however since this is already our primary consideration, the next most important differential would be Metastases. Lytic metastases typically occur in older patients, appear permeative and destructive, and lack the characteristic multiloculated fluid-fluid levels. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Osteoblastoma, given it also frequently arises in the posterior elements of the spine in young patients and its presentation can overlap with secondary ABCs. However, osteoblastomas typically demonstrate an osteoid matrix and are characteristically surrounded by extensive peritumoral oedema that extends well beyond the boundaries of the lesion.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to define the full anatomical extent of the lesion, specifically assessing for epidural extension, spinal cord compression, and the integrity of the vertebral body to prevent pathological fracture. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is essential to establish the growth rate of the lesion and identify any preexisting osseous lesions, as up to one-third of ABCs are secondary to another underlying bone tumour. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the whole spine, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely demonstrating the characteristic fluid-fluid levels of varying signal intensities and accurately evaluating any spinal cord or nerve root compression. A non-contrast CT of the spine would be highly beneficial to precisely define the delicate bony septations, the extent of focal cortical destruction, and aid in surgical planning. I would specify T1-weighted and T2-weighted sequences to demonstrate the fluid-fluid levels—where extracellular methaemoglobin in the supernatant is high signal on T1, contrasting with low signal intracellular methaemoglobin in dependent red blood cells—along with STIR sequences to assess local oedema. If the diagnosis remains in doubt or a secondary underlying lesion is suspected, I would specify a CT-guided percutaneous biopsy, targeting the peripheral solid-tissue component or septations to avoid the non-diagnostic necrotic or haemorrhagic centre. This should go to the Spinal Surgery and Sarcoma MDT. My report would clearly document the degree of spinal canal stenosis, the presence of any cord compression, and the exact vertebral levels involved to guide definitive surgical curettage and spinal stabilisation.
